chunk_summary: This section discusses molecular diagnostics, whole-body MRI, and culture-negative pathways in diagnosing bone infections, emphasizing advanced imaging and targeted microbiological techniques for complex or culture-negative cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of molecular diagnostics, including PCR and 16S ribosomal assays, in culture-negative bone infections.
Learn the indications and utility of whole-body MRI in multifocal or complex osteomyelitis cases.
Recognize the importance of clinical response and repeat sampling in managing persistent symptoms despite negative cultures.
key_facts: Targeted PCR assays can detect Kingella kingae, mycobacteria, and resistance determinants in culture-negative cases.
Broad-range bacterial sequencing, such as 16S ribosomal assays, is particularly useful in preschool children and immunocompromised patients.
Whole-body MRI is valuable for identifying multifocal osteomyelitis, chronic nonbacterial osteomyelitis, or occult second sites.
Ultrasound-guided aspiration is effective for deep but accessible collections, especially around the hip and soft tissues.
If cultures are negative but the child is improving, continue a focused regimen and monitor CRP trends.
reference_quote: PIDS/IDSA 2023 Guidelines recommend targeted PCR and advanced imaging for culture-negative osteomyelitis, particularly in pediatric cases. (Source: https://www.idsociety.org/practice-guideline/bone-...)
document_key_topics: Role of whole-body MRI in multifocal osteomyelitis
Management strategies for persistent symptoms in bone infections
